script:
Hello, I am Josh Weinberg and this is The Portfolio Podcast. Thursday, as of July 2 2026 at 4:28 AM ET — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio closed at one hundred two thousand seven hundred thirty-three dollars and forty-six cents, up two thousand seven hundred thirty-three dollars and forty-six cents on the session. That is a gain of two point seven three percent. A strong day across most of the book.

Meta Platforms did something Thursday that does not happen often. A single session. Eight point nine four percent. Fifty dollars and thirty-one cents added to the share price in one trading day. That kind of move on a mega-cap does not come from noise. It comes from conviction. And the numbers behind it are worth your full attention.

No broad macro data was available as of this recording at 4:28 AM ET on Thursday. What is clear from the price action is that technology and communication services led the session, with the Nasdaq Composite posting a strong positive day. The moves in Apple, A-A-P-L, Microsoft, M-S-F-T, and Meta, M-E-T-A, were broad enough to suggest this was not a single-stock story — there was sector-wide buying pressure in large-cap tech. Rotation into mega-cap names appeared orderly and sustained through the close.

Meta Platforms, M-E-T-A, is the top story of Thursday's session, and it is not close. The stock closed at six hundred twelve dollars and ninety-one cents, up fifty dollars and thirty-one cents, a gain of eight point nine four percent. That is an extraordinary single-session move for a company with a market capitalization of this scale. Meta carries a four point four eight percent weighting in the Q-Q-Q-M portfolio, and Thursday's move was the single largest dollar and percentage contributor to the portfolio's overall gain on the session. While no single headline in Thursday's news feed directly attributed the move to a specific catalyst, the broader narrative around Meta has been building. The company has been aggressively repositioning itself around AI-driven advertising infrastructure and its Llama model ecosystem. Analyst sentiment heading into the second half of twenty twenty-six has been constructive, with the stock recovering meaningfully from the broader Magnificent Seven rotation that, according to a Yahoo Finance report published at 1:30 AM ET Thursday, saw big tech suffer its worst month in years this past June. Meta's move suggests institutional money is returning to the name with urgency. For Q-Q-Q-M holders, this is a position that now carries more embedded gain, and the key question going forward is whether Thursday's move was a one-session relief rally or the beginning of a more sustained re-rating. Watch second quarter earnings guidance language closely.

Now the full holdings rundown, starting with the largest positions.

Apple, A-A-P-L, closed at two hundred ninety-four dollars and thirty-eight cents, up twelve dollars and sixty-four cents, a gain of four point four nine percent on Thursday's session. That is the largest weighting in this portfolio at eight point nine seven percent, and it delivered. Two pieces of news frame the Apple story right now. Seeking Alpha, publishing at 3:05 AM ET Thursday, reiterated a buy on A-A-P-L, citing margin resilience amid chip shortages, third quarter twenty twenty-six revenue and earnings per share expectations, and Siri AI as a forward catalyst. The bullish signal score on that piece was zero point one nine — moderate, not euphoric. The second story, from Yahoo Finance at 1:30 AM ET, raised the broader question of whether Wall Street has fallen out of love with the Magnificent Seven after June's rough month. That tension — a reiterated buy against a narrative of institutional rotation away — is exactly the kind of setup that makes A-A-P-L worth monitoring closely. A third item from Yahoo Finance at midnight flagged a hidden risk in Apple's India supply chain shift: the strategy reduces China dependency but may be creating new concentration risk in Indian manufacturing. The stock moved well Thursday, but these structural questions do not resolve in a single session.

Turning to Microsoft, M-S-F-T. The stock closed at three hundred eighty-four dollars and twenty-eight cents, up fifteen dollars and seventy-one cents, a gain of four point two six percent. Microsoft carries an eight point four two percent weight in this portfolio and contributed meaningfully to Thursday's gain. The news backdrop for M-S-F-T on Thursday was dominated by a question that is now central to the entire AI trade. A Yahoo Finance report published at 3:00 AM ET Thursday described what it called the pricing crisis clouding the AI trade — the problem that tokens, the currency of the AI economy, have no agreed-upon valuation. Customers are confused, and Wall Street is puzzled. That is not a comfortable headline for a company whose forward revenue story is increasingly tied to Azure AI monetization. Separately, Microsoft was mentioned in a Yahoo Finance piece about India becoming a data center hub, noting that Amazon, M-S-F-T, and Google facilities are being approved under ordinary environmental policy channels in India. That is a quiet but meaningful infrastructure build. Watch Azure revenue growth rates in the next earnings cycle. That number will determine whether Thursday's move was justified.

Over at Nvidia, N-V-D-A. The stock closed at one hundred ninety-seven dollars and fifty-eight cents, up two dollars and sixty-one cents, a gain of one point three four percent. N-V-D-A carries an eight point one five percent weight in this portfolio. The gain was real but modest relative to the broader tech rally on Thursday. None of the news items tagged to N-V-D-A in Thursday's feed were directly relevant to the company — the headlines covered SpaceX, Realty Income, Palantir, and a fallen high-growth stock. Nvidia traded on its own momentum Thursday. The relative underperformance versus Apple, A-A-P-L, and Microsoft, M-S-F-T, is worth noting. After a sustained run, N-V-D-A may be in a consolidation phase. The next meaningful catalyst is data center order flow commentary from hyperscalers.

Alphabet, G-O-O-G-L, closed at three hundred sixty-one dollars and twenty-one cents, up seven dollars and fifty-six cents, a gain of two point one four percent. G-O-O-G-L is eight point zero one percent of this portfolio. Thursday brought a significant legal development. According to Yahoo Finance at 3:40 AM ET, Alphabet lost its fight against a record four point one billion euro EU antitrust fine — originally four point three four billion euros — imposed in twenty eighteen for using the Android operating system to block rivals and force pre-installation of Google Search, Chrome, and the Play Store. The Court of Justice of the European Union upheld the ruling. This is a headline that matters beyond the fine itself. It signals continued regulatory pressure on Google's core distribution model in Europe, and it is described as likely to boost Europe's broader crackdown on big tech. The stock still gained two point one four percent Thursday, suggesting markets had already priced some of this risk. But the legal overhang is not resolved.

Amazon, A-M-Z-N, closed at two hundred forty-one dollars and seventy cents, up one dollar and fifty-six cents, a gain of zero point six five percent. A-M-Z-N carries a five point five two percent weight in the portfolio. Thursday's move was the softest among the gainers in this book. A-M-Z-N was referenced in the India data center piece alongside Microsoft and Google, noting its infrastructure expansion in that market. No earnings or product news drove the session. The muted gain relative to peers may reflect ongoing investor debate about the pace of A-W-S monetization and consumer spending durability. Retail and cloud both matter here, and neither had a specific catalyst Thursday.

Meanwhile, Broadcom, A-V-G-O, was the first decliner in Thursday's session. The stock closed at three hundred sixty-nine dollars and thirty-four cents, down three dollars and eleven cents, a loss of zero point eight four percent. A-V-G-O represents four point six one percent of this portfolio. No specific news drove the move. In a session where most of the book gained, A-V-G-O's decline stands out. Custom AI chip demand from hyperscalers remains the forward thesis for this name, and with no catalyst in either direction Thursday, the slight pullback likely reflects profit-taking after a strong run.

Turning to Tesla, T-S-L-A. The stock closed at four hundred twenty-five dollars and thirty cents, up thirteen dollars and forty-six cents, a gain of three point two seven percent. T-S-L-A is three point five four percent of this portfolio. No specific news drove the move in Thursday's feed. The gain was solid and consistent with a broader risk-on session in consumer discretionary. Delivery data and any commentary around the energy and robotaxi businesses remain the catalysts to watch in the weeks ahead.

Finally, Costco, C-O-S-T, was the second and sharper decliner on Thursday. The stock closed at nine hundred twenty-four dollars and sixty-seven cents, down twenty-two dollars and one cent, a loss of two point three two percent. C-O-S-T carries a two point eight one percent weight in this portfolio. No specific news was tagged to Costco in Thursday's session. The decline in a risk-on environment is consistent with sector rotation away from defensive consumer staples names. C-O-S-T has had a strong run, and some giveback on a day when growth names led is not unusual. Monthly sales data remains the near-term fundamental check for this position.

Three numbers to watch in the next twenty-four to forty-eight hours. First, any Federal Reserve speaker commentary over the July 4th holiday weekend, given the ongoing token pricing debate around AI monetization flagged in Thursday's Microsoft, M-S-F-T, news — clarity on rate trajectory matters for growth stock valuations. Second, watch for any follow-through EU regulatory action after the Google, G-O-O-G-L, Android antitrust ruling — additional enforcement actions could surface quickly. Third, monitor Apple, A-A-P-L, supply chain headlines out of India and China, given the Seeking Alpha buy reiteration against the disclosed manufacturing risk flagged at midnight Thursday.

That is your Thursday, July 2 twenty twenty-six recap. The portfolio is up two point seven three percent. Next episode drops at 5 PM ET on the next trading day. Watch Meta, M-E-T-A, for whether Thursday's eight point nine four percent move holds — that single name will tell you a great deal about where institutional conviction in this market actually sits. I am Josh Weinberg. This is The Portfolio Podcast.
